title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered for community gardening projects. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Maya, The Rooftop Grower
When a few residents won permission to use the roof of their apartment block, Maya took on the role of coordinator, although she had never run anything similar before. [47]She says the first obstacle was not the planting itself, but persuading the caretaker to unlock the roof at the same time every Saturday.[/47] [48]Her job in finance helped more than she had expected, because she created a shared spreadsheet for watering duties, spending and seed orders.[/48] At first, people laughed at how formal it looked, but later they admitted it prevented arguments. What pleased her most was seeing a neighbour who had barely spoken to anyone before begin bringing tea and staying to chat. Maya admits the group almost failed by trying to plant too much in its first season, especially tomatoes that needed constant attention. Now she believes a community garden depends less on enthusiasm than on small routines that people can actually keep.
texts.B:
### Tom, The School Garden Cook
[49]Tom, a retired chef, was asked to help revive the vegetable beds at a local school after the headteacher met him at a Sunday market.[/49] He accepted because he missed the noise and energy of a busy kitchen. [50]He was astonished by how much paperwork was needed before the children could use even simple gardening tools.[/50] Rather than growing fashionable crops, he focused on herbs and vegetables that could later be turned into lunch, such as parsley, beans and potatoes. His favourite moment came when a class who assumed carrots arrived ready-washed in plastic bags pulled their own from the soil. Tom says his cooking experience allowed him to turn small harvests into something pupils genuinely wanted to taste, instead of a worthy but dull lesson. He measures success by the parents who now wait after school swapping recipes near the gate, and by pupils asking whether the soup contains “their” onions.
texts.C:
### Lina, The Station Planter
Lina, a graphic designer, started with three planters outside the railway station after the station manager gave her some old barrels that were going to be thrown away. [51]Because her trains were often delayed, she had spent months noticing commuters staring at the same grey fence.[/51] She used her design skills to choose flowers that looked good from a moving platform and [55]to make handwritten labels easy to read at a glance.[/55] The labels included simple names, not Latin ones, because she wanted people to feel invited rather than tested. [52]The most discouraging moment was discovering that a whole row of young plants had been stolen overnight.[/52] She nearly stopped then, but was surprised by how protective passers-by became, with one cyclist regularly reporting broken branches. For Lina, the project proved that even a tiny neglected corner can change how people feel about a daily journey, especially when they notice it slowly improving.
texts.D:
### Omar, The Hospital Courtyard Gardener
[53]Omar volunteered to improve the courtyard behind a hospital where his wife had recovered after an operation.[/53] He wanted patients to have a view of something other than the car park, but he quickly realised that good intentions were not enough. Unlike the others, he had no gardening experience and learned by listening carefully to nurses who knew which smells could make patients feel nauseous. [54]His hardest task was arranging quiet work sessions so that drilling, digging and wheelbarrows did not disturb the nearby wards.[/54] He was deeply moved when a physiotherapist began using the path around the flowerbeds with patients taking their first steps outside. Omar says patience mattered more than speed, because some weeks only a few minutes of work were possible between hospital routines. [56]He now plans to train a replacement rather than let the project depend on him.[/56] He thinks the garden should feel permanent, not personal.

questions.question: mentions having to solve a regular access problem before the work could take place?
questions.explanation: Maya says the first obstacle was persuading the caretaker to unlock the roof every Saturday.

questions.question: says their professional background helped them organise practical information for the project?
questions.explanation: Maya used her finance background to create a shared spreadsheet for watering, spending and seed orders.

questions.question: was personally invited to join the project by someone connected with the place where it happened?
questions.explanation: Tom joined after the headteacher asked him to help revive the school vegetable beds.

questions.question: was surprised by the amount of official preparation required before participants could use equipment?
questions.explanation: Tom was astonished by the paperwork needed before children could use simple gardening tools.

questions.question: explains that the idea came from repeatedly noticing an unattractive place while waiting?
questions.explanation: Lina had often noticed commuters staring at the grey fence while her trains were delayed.

questions.question: describes feeling discouraged after something was removed from the project site?
questions.explanation: Lina says a whole row of young plants was stolen overnight.

questions.question: became involved because of a personal connection with people using a medical setting?
questions.explanation: Omar volunteered at the hospital courtyard where his wife had recovered after an operation.

questions.question: had to organise the work in a way that avoided disturbing people nearby?
questions.explanation: Omar arranged quiet work sessions so that noise would not disturb the hospital wards.

questions.question: mentions designing written information so that people could understand it quickly?
questions.explanation: Lina made handwritten labels easy to read at a glance.

questions.question: says the project should not become dependent on a single person in the future?
questions.explanation: Omar plans to train a replacement rather than let the project depend on him.
